sentences.text: There were placards on buildings with the excerpts of a newspaper called the which was a newspaper edited by a man by the name of Streicher, that had very, very nasty cartoons about Jews and terrible anti-Semitic slurs.
And they were enlarged three stories high and pasted onto sides of buildings.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was really the first thing.
Then there were all kinds of marches.
Austria had something like 47 different political parties [BACKROUND NOISE] and they were always having marches and torchlight parades and things like that.
And of course, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during the civil war, the various factions were actually shooting at each other and we had martial law.
And we were locked into our apartments and couldn't get out.
And we didn't have any water.
We didn't have any electricity.
I mean-- then they burnt the Palace of Justice down.
I mean, it-- it was constant strife.
I don't know all the dates anymore.
But I would say from 1934 on.
Well, we heard a lot about it, but it was all rumors.
We didn't really know as much as we should have known or-- we read a lot in the paper.
And we didn't believe it.
We didn't want to believe it.
We heard about-- German Jewish refugees came into Austria and told stories.
And we felt sorry for them and my father donated money toward them and all that.
But we didn't believe that anything could happen like the death camps.
We really didn't believe that.
We heard about people getting shoeboxes full of ashes from their relatives.
And of course, after Hitler came, my uncles were arrested.
My father had to constantly hide.
The janitor of our building was kindly disposed toward him and he would come into the store and he said, hey, they're coming.
The uniformed SS or the SA man would come.
So my father would run out the back door and hide and things like that.
I mean, you could hardly not notice it.
And there were constant stories about a cousin having been beaten at school.
He was going to medical school and he was beaten at the university.
And-- and people tried to cross the border and were caught and were-- and came back.
And there are so many stories that you just couldn't miss it.
And there was fear.
And I only tried to get out.
That was my big stress, you know.
That was my hobby, to try to get myself out of Austria.
Well, since I left fairly early in-- I left in August '38.
And Hitler marched in in March.
When I left, my father still had the store.
We still had the apartment.
Right after I left, all that was taken away from them.
They had to move into a coal bin in the basement with other Jewish families from other buildings.
And so I didn't see that part of it.
The way I got out was that I went to the various consulates and looked into telephone books by people-- about people of my own name, Harmel.
Harmel is not a common name.
And at the American consulate, I found five people by that name in phonebooks in New York, Cleveland, and Washington.
And I wrote letters to them.
And at that time, I was 16 years old.
And I wrote five letters telling them that I hope that they know the situation in Austria, that I was a Jewish girl, that I had good grades in school and that I was healthy, and I need help.
All I need is an affidavit of support.
I don't need any money.

I have enough money for fare and I have enough clothes and that I won't bother them or be a burden to them, but I need an affidavit of support in order to get a visa and the permission to come to the United States.
And by return mail, I had three affidavits, which was a-- nothing short of a miracle.
And they either thought that we were really related or manufactured a relationship for the benefit of the United States State Department.
And it became like a re-unification of a family.
And I then had a visa and an affidavit to come to the United States.
However, the quota number was so high that I had to wait and I couldn't actually get the permit to leave.
I had to wait 'til my quota number was up.
And I would have had to wait so long it would have been too late.
So I forced my way into the consulate in Vienna after standing in line all night long, and-- something like 14 hours.
And by that time, I was so overwrought that I screamed at the man who was at the door and pushed him aside and ran up the stairs and got into a room and started to cry.
And a woman came in and asked me why I was crying.
And it's a long story, but she happened to be the wife of the General Consul of the American-- of the United States.
She then ushered me into his office.
They didn't speak any German.
And somehow I made them understand that my parents were about to leave for Cyprus and that I refused to go, because I didn't want to go to Israel.
I was not a Zionist.
I only wanted to go to the United States and I had a visa and I had an affidavit and can't they hurry it up and let me get out, because I was going to be left all alone.
My parents were about to leave and I didn't want to be left alone in Vienna.
So the consul got an interpreter and told me that a friend of his was coming in to Vienna and I should write him a letter.
He gave me the name.
His name was Alfred Jaretzki.
He didn't tell me who he was.
He just said he's a man that's coming from New York.
Write to him and he may be able to help you.
He says, send the letter to me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he may help you.
And two days later, we had a telephone call that Mr.
Jaretzki indeed was in Vienna and wanted to see me.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh.
I thought he turned it off.
And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
Mr.
Jaretzki?
He was-- I didn't know that at the time.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
He happened to be head of the Joint Distribution Committee in New York, which I didn't know.
And after he saw me and we talked to each other, he introduced me to a young woman whose name was Ruth Fellner.
And she was head of the Jewish Children's Refugee Committee in London, at the Woburn House.
And they said, would you like to come to England and wait for your visa to come through in England?
And I said yes, but I can't go to England.
He says, we'll arrange that.
And miraculously, they arranged overnight to get a visa to go to England and I flew in a Czech plane with Mr.
Jaretzki to London two days later.
My parents didn't want to let me go, because they thought he might be a white slaver.
And-- but they made inquiries at the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], which is the cultural organization, a Jewish organization in Vienna.
And they said, it's perfectly all right.
He is with the Joint Distribution Committee and your daughter is safe and very lucky to be able to go with him.
My parents donated money to him for the cause of helping other people in my plight or their plight.
And we didn't have to pay.
This was a voluntary action on my parents' part, because at that time they had all the money that you would need.
And they bought me a ticket on the with an open departure, first class ticket on the from London to New York, with departure open.
So he took me to England.
We arrived in London.
And there was Miss Fellner who took me to Woburn House and made arrangements for me to live in a boarding house in London.
I'd like to stop right there and go back to that.
I wanted to find out a couple things.
When you were in Austria, did you ever see any book burnings, anything like that?
Oh, yes.
That was very common.
There were mountains of book piled in the courtyard right where I lived and they burnt books at major public places.
We were caught on the street many times and had to scrub swastikas off the sidewalk.
There were always gangs of SA youths.
They were young people in these brown and red uniforms.
And they would just catch you and make you do things.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did that twice, while other people walked by and screamed at you or spit at you or laughed.
And it was absolutely horrible, but nothing compared to what other people went through.
How did-- how does one feel?
Terrible.
Terrible.
The only thing that saved me-- and I must say it's probably the best thing.
According to my aunts and uncle, I was a very spoiled brat.
My parents thought that I was God's gift to this earth.
And that helped me I knew that-- I knew that I was all right and that they were wrong.
And it didn't break my spirit.
And I think that was because my parents made me feel that way, nobody could hurt me.
I did not lose my self-esteem.
My father kept saying it's going to blow over.
And they thought in the beginning that I was a hysterical teenager and I was making too much of it.
My father says, I was born here.
I was in the war.
I was a ardent Austrian.
I was patriotic.
How can anything happen to me?
Do you know what your father did during World War I?
He was a soldier.
I don't know too much about it.
No.
Nobody got favors.
[LAUGHS]
I mean, the the defeat was so tremendous that it-- it was a miracle that any of them survived.
The battles were so terrible, especially on the border of Italy, that in the-- at the last moment, from what I have heard, they took 15- and 16-year-old boys and 70-year-old men.
I mean, it was called the [NON-ENGLISH], the last effort, you know, where they took anybody that could still walk.
No.
He got this good job, you know, as a traffic manager, which was a very good job.
But my father was very educated and extremely verbal and personable, so-- but he didn't have much common sense when it came to the time.
Well, he didn't want to leave.
Well, like I said, my only occupation at that time was to find a way to get out of Austria.
That was a constant quest.
It took every day walking on the streets being afraid that you would be accosted or taken to jail or murdered or goodness knows what.
My mother never knew whether I was ever going to come home again after I left in the morning.
And I would talk to some other young people that were doing the same thing.
No.
They-- there was really no chance for them to go with me.
When I got the affidavits, that was only for me.
Because I was young, these people whose name was Harmel-- it turned out that they were all related to each other.
And they knew that the other people had also sent affidavits.
And then they combined and they forwarded their affidavits to a daughter of the man in Washington, whose husband was a famous patent attorney in Chicago.
And they had a daughter my age, so they thought it would be perfect if I would come and be a ready-made sister for her.
But when we asked, would they bring my parents out?
They said, no.
They didn't want to take the responsibility of older people, and they only wanted to do that for me.
Well, as it happened, I left for England in August '38.
My parents were left in Vienna.
The visa to Cyprus never came through.
And then I finally ended up in a foster home of people that were recommended by the Quakers.
And they eventually brought my parents out of Austria.
They sent for them and they got them to be domestic servants in England.
That was the only way that anybody could immigrate to England.
You had to be a domestic servant.
You couldn't do any other kind of work.
And that's how my parents were saved.
And they came to England on the last train out of Austria.
Before the war, in 1939.
August, August 15th.
Jaretzki.
Came to London.
And then I stayed in a boarding house where the Jewish Children's Refugee Committee paid for me to live in that boarding house, which was next door to where Sigmund Freud lived.
I had a cousin who was born in England and lived in Austria and also fled.
But because she was an British citizen, could get out right away.
And they lived in this part of London, in Swiss Cottage.
And her husband was a psychiatrist and had studied with Sigmund Freud and was a very close friend of Anna Freud.
So he introduced me.
And I saw him every day for the few weeks that I lived there when he took a walk on the arm of his daughter, Anna.
And at that time, he had a very bad problem with his tongue.
I think he had cancer of the tongue and it was hard for him to speak.
What was the boarding house like and where was it?
Well, it was in a part called Swiss Cottage, which is a nice part of London.
And it had all-- sort of a motley crew of people living there.
And you all had breakfast together.
And they served-- and I had a tiny, tiny room.
And I came to breakfast and they served this watery-looking coffee and it was the strangest coffee I ever had.
And I said, that is a-- what is this?
And they said it was tea.
It was tea with milk.
I had never seen that before.
So it was all right.
I had things to do every day, such as going to the Children's Refugee Committee to see how they-- where they would place me and what they would do with me.
And at that time, Chamberlain went to Munich to talk to Hitler.
And he came back with peace in our time, you know, the man with the umbrella.
And gas masks were handed out to everybody.
And the children had to be evacuated out of London.
And then I became an evacuee.
And Miss Fellner saw to it that I was placed with a nice family in the country after I had spent three months with-- training at a children's nursery.
The children's nursery where they put me in training to become a children's nurse was called the Lady Cynthia Moseley Day Nursery.
Lady Cynthia Mosley was the dead wife of Sir Oswald Mosley, the English Nazi.
And the matron there was so terrible.
She told me that Hitler was a wonderful man and that he was absolutely right in what he was doing to the Jews.
And because I had been raised with maids and I didn't know anything-- I didn't know how to iron.
I didn't know how to scrub a floor.
I didn't know anything.
So they thought I was very stupid and very useless.
And she said that people like me should be eliminated, because I was just a useless creature.
So I was so miserable and so unhappy--
I don't remember her name.
All I know is I didn't speak English at the time.
And in the few weeks that I was there, I went to bed with a dictionary every night just so that I could learn enough to tell her what I thought of her.
And I escaped by squeezing myself out through a toilet window.
And I had a little diamond ring that I gave to a greengrocer across the street so he gave me carfare.
And I went to the Jewish Refugees Committee and I saw Miss Fellner after waiting for hours, all in my uniform because I had to wear a uniform.
And said, I'd rather go back to Vienna and go to a concentration camp than stay in that nursery, because it's just like a concentration camp.
Of course, now that I'm used to scrubbing and cleaning, it wouldn't be so bad.
But at that time, I just felt terrible.
And I was alone and I didn't speak English.
And all the other young nurses put all the dirty work on me.
And everybody was making fun of me because I didn't know anything.
So she then turned me over to the Society of Friends, the Quakers.
And they found me a foster home in the country, because they wanted to get me out of London by then.
When you were in the boarding school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how many people were there?
In the boarding house?
I don't remember.
Maybe 10.
It was like a little bed and breakfast place.
Yes.
I had my own room.
I had a tiny little room.
And I had seven suitcases.
And my mother packed in a hurry, because it-- I only had two days when Mr.
Jaretzki got me the visa.
So she packed.
I had seven formals, because she thought that they dressed for dinner in England, and 30 pairs of underpants, but nothing really useful.
[LAUGHS] So I had all the wrong things with me.
And that's how I came to that boarding house.
What was the nationality of the people in the boarding house?
I really don't remember.
I don't remember.
Don't forget, I couldn't communicate.
About three weeks.
My parents didn't want to let me go.
It was a tremendous struggle to make them let me go.
And we took a taxi to the airport.
None of us had ever been on an airplane before.
This was in 1938.
People weren't flying very much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- neither one of us said anything.
We just kissed and hugged and-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And I remember looking out the window of the plane and seeing them get smaller and smaller.
[CRYING] Anyway, it's so long ago.
But it feels like yesterday.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then I was so enthralled with the idea of flying and it was so beautiful to be above the clouds and it was exciting.
Everything was an adventure after that.
When I was in England, I could telephone them and write to them.
And they sent me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] postal money orders so I could get a little money out of it and pocket money.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, of course, I was lucky enough to have them come later on.
And the people in England, my foster homes, were very wonderful people.
And they were very nice to me.
But I had to move 18 times, because none of them thought I would be there.
It was-- I had a transit visa for three months.
But my-- to-- to England.
[BACKROUND NOISE] But my American visa didn't come through, because the war broke out.
And during the war, there was no way of getting across.
But I left in November 1939.
The war had broken out in September.
So I went on a French ship.
By that time, the was sinking in New York Harbor.
It was tilting.
And the French Line didn't want to give me any passage.
They said that the ticket that I had the money was in Vienna and they never got that money.
The Refugee Committee then supplied me with a lawyer.
And they fought and said, you got the money out right away, which they did.
And they gave me a passage on the smallest French Line ship, the instead of the largest.
And it was a blacked-out ship all painted gray with guns mounted on the decks.
And it was a gunrunner, really.
And it took 21 days to come across the Atlantic, while mines were swept loose and U-boats attacked.
We were under constant U-boat attack.
Then we got to New York Harbor safely.
And again, I had to leave my parents behind, because by then, they were as domestic servants in a home of a colonel close to where I lived.
And I got them that position, which turned out to be a very bad idea, because my parents were then considered German nationals and they were working for an English colonel.
And the colonel got in trouble because they were employing German nationals, so my father was arrested and taken to a camp on the Isle of Man.
And then later on my mother was arrested.
And they both were in different camps on the Isle of Man.
And the only communication they had with me was-- when I was in the States already was by V-mail, which was just a letter of 11 lines that they sent to me.
And then I could write to them and tell them where the other was.
They didn't know about each other.
V-mail was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] a letter that was censored.
And it was on one page and could only be 11 lines long.
And most of that they cut out.
It was always censored.
Oh, yes.
Of course.
Of course.
My first foster home was with people by the name of Rosevere.
They were-- he was-- had just joined the Quakers.
And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
They had just come back from having been in Burma for 17 years, where he was a civil engineer.
